preFix.title: Re-grouting bathroom tiles
preFix.subtitle: Remove failed grout and apply fresh, waterproof grout
preFix.excerpt: How to remove old, cracked, or mouldy grout from bathroom tiles and apply fresh grout: raking out, cleaning the joint, mixing and applying new grout, finishing the surface, and sealing the joint at bath and shower edges.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Wall and Floor Tiling syllabus. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Grout in a bathroom fails in two ways: it cracks with tile movement or substrate settlement, and it discolours through mould growth in the damp joint. Cracked grout allows water behind the tiles; mouldy grout is unsightly and may indicate a deeper moisture problem. Both are remedied the same way: rake out the old grout, clean the joint, apply fresh grout. The job takes a morning for a medium bathroom wall, and the result lasts a decade on a well-prepared surface.
You need a grout rake (or an oscillating multi-tool with a grout blade), a utility knife, a sponge, clean water, waterproof tile grout, a
or silicone sealant for the bath-to-tile joint. Wear gloves and safety glasses while raking.
Step 1, rake out the old grout

Work along each joint with a grout rake, applying steady forward pressure. A manual grout rake is a carbide-tipped tool that fits between tiles and pulls the old grout out; an oscillating multi-tool with a grout blade is faster on large areas. The objective is to remove grout to a depth of at least 3 mm so the fresh grout has something to key into. Do not force the tool sideways at the end of a run; tile edges chip easily.

At internal corners and at the joint between tiles and the bath or shower tray, rake out the grout and remove any old silicone sealant with the utility knife. These joints will be re-sealed with flexible sealant rather than rigid grout.

Vacuum loose dust from the joints. Wipe the tile faces with a damp sponge, then allow the wall to dry for at least 2 hours. On areas with black mould, apply an anti-mould bathroom cleaner and leave for 10 minutes before rinsing. If the mould growth is extensive and recurs regularly, there may be a moisture problem behind the tiles that requires investigation before re-grouting.
Step 3, mix and apply fresh grout

Mix powdered grout to a smooth thick paste by adding water a little at a time, stirring constantly. The consistency should hold a shape on the edge of the float without slumping. Pre-mixed grout is ready to use. Load the grout float and spread grout diagonally across the joints, pressing firmly into each joint with the edge of the float. Work in sections of about 1 square metre at a time so the surface does not skin before you clean it.

Wait 10 to 15 minutes for the grout to firm up slightly (it should no longer be wet-shiny), then wipe the tile faces with a damp sponge, rinsing the sponge frequently. Work with a near-dry sponge: too much water pulls grout out of the joints. Clean in broad circular strokes first to remove the bulk, then with a damp cloth to polish the haze off the tile face. Allow to dry overnight before the next step.

Run masking tape parallel to both tile edges of the joint, leaving a 4 to 5 mm gap for the sealant. Cut the sealant cartridge nozzle at 45 degrees and apply a continuous bead into the joint. Smooth with a wet finger in one firm stroke, removing the excess immediately. Peel the masking tape while the sealant is still wet and allow to cure for 24 hours before the bath or shower is used.

attrs.fix: Apply a proprietary grout haze remover with a soft cloth; leave for 5 minutes and wipe off. Rinse thoroughly.
attrs.cause: Grout allowed to dry too long before wiping, or tiles have a textured or porous surface.
attrs.symptom: Grout haze left on tile face after cleaning

attrs.fix: Rake out the cracked grout and investigate the cause of movement. Use flexible grout or silicone sealant at any joint where movement is present.
attrs.cause: The substrate is moving; the joint was not raked deeply enough; or rigid grout was used at a movement joint.
attrs.symptom: New grout cracking within a few weeks

attrs.fix: Improve ventilation (extractor fan on a timer or humidity-sensing model). Check for moisture behind the tiles before re-grouting again.
attrs.cause: Inadequate bathroom ventilation keeping humidity high, or a water ingress problem behind the tiles.
attrs.symptom: Mould returning quickly on new grout
